title: Te Whakamahi i te Mātauranga Taketake ki te Hoahoa Whare: Applying Indigenous Knowledge to Shelter Design
subject: Technology
culturalContext: Students will explore how Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau (stories) of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream) and traditional whare construction principles inform sustainable, culturally-responsive shelter design. They will investigate how their ancestors used local resources like harakeke, raupō, and tōtara from the stream's ecosystem to create structures that were both functional and deeply connected to the whenua.
learningObjectives: Analyze and document at least three traditional Ngāti Kahungunu building techniques used along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Design a scale model of a sustainable shelter that incorporates both traditional Māori principles and modern materials, specifically addressing the local climate and environment
Create a digital presentation that explains how their design reflects Ngāti Kahungunu values, such as kaitiakitanga (guardianship) and whanaungatanga (relationships)
Critically evaluate their own and a peer's design against a set of criteria including cultural integrity, sustainability, and functionality

activities.title: He Haerenga: A Journey to the Awa
activities.description: A guided field trip to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with a local kaumātua (elder). Students will observe the traditional resources (harakeke, raupō) and hear pūrākau (stories) connected to the place, focusing on how the environment provided for physical and spiritual shelter. They will sketch and photograph key features and resources.

activities.title: Te Whare Taketake: Deconstructing the Traditional Whare
activities.description: A hands-on workshop, potentially with a local carver or weaver, analyzing the components, structure, and symbolism of a traditional whare. Students will create detailed technical drawings of elements like the heke (rafters), poupou (carved posts), and their connections, linking each to its cultural and environmental significance.

activities.title: Te Hoahoa: From Pūrākau to Blueprint
activities.description: Using Tinkercad or similar CAD software, students will work in pairs to design a modern whare or shelter inspired by their research. Their digital model must integrate at least one traditional design principle (e.g., orientation, use of sustainable materials, symbolic design) and one modern sustainable technology (e.g., solar panels, rainwater collection).

activities.title: Te Wānanga: Design Critique Circle
activities.description: A structured peer-assessment session using a co-constructed rubric. Students present their digital models and receive feedback focused on the strength of the cultural connection, the practicality of the sustainable technology, and the overall aesthetic and functional design.

activities.title: Te Whakaaturanga: Exhibition for Whānau
activities.description: Students prepare a final display of their technical drawings, digital models, and a brief written or recorded explanation of their design journey. This is presented to whānau and community experts, explaining how their work embodies the concept of kaitiakitanga for Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.

resources: A local Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua or knowledge holder (e.g., from the local marae) to guide the field trip and share pūrākau
Digital access to the 'Ngāi Tahu Mahinga Kai' interactive website for exploring traditional resource use
Tinkercad (free online CAD software) for 3D modelling
The book 'Māori Architecture: From Fale to Wharenui and Beyond' by Deidre Brown for research

tasks: A portfolio containing: field trip sketches/notes, technical drawings of a traditional whare component, and screenshots of the final 3D digital model
A 3-minute digital presentation (e.g., Flipgrid video) explaining the cultural and sustainable features of their designed shelter
A written peer-feedback review for one other student's design

nzcAlignment: Technology: Technological Practice (Level 4 - Planning for Practice; Brief Development)
Technology: Nature of Technology (Level 4 - Characteristics of Technology)
Social Sciences: Place and Environment (Level 4 - Understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges)
